input_ontology.justification: DrBenchmark's task taxonomy (POS, NER, MCQA, MCC, MLC, STS) [Q81] is calibrated for clinical and biomedical research NLP rather than the EU regulatory workflow. The deployment requires NER and STS over EMA/ANSM regulatory document genres (SmPCs, CTD modules, EU-RMPs, PILs), and these document categories do not appear as explicit task categories in the benchmark. While QUAERO EMEA and Mantra-GSC EMEA/Patents provide partial PIL/patent overlap [Q34, Q40], the majority of tasks (FrenchMedMCQA, MORFITT, DiaMED, CAS POS, ESSAI POS, E3C) involve task types or genres orthogonal to the deployment's NER/STS regulatory compliance functions. This is a HIGH-priority dimension per the elicitation, and the gap is substantial — though not total, since QUAERO and CLISTER provide partial NER and STS coverage on biomedical French.
strengths: Task inventory does include both NER and STS — the two task types the deployment needs [Q1, Q4, Q18]
QUAERO EMEA NER provides authentic EMA package leaflet text in regulatory register, the only confirmed regulatory-genre source (QUAERO-D1, QUAERO-D2, QUAERO-D14)
Mantra-GSC includes EMEA and Patents NER configurations that are documented as deployment-relevant genres [Q40]
The benchmark's diversity across 20 tasks gives broad signal for French biomedical model selection [Q83]

checklist_responses.IO-1: The deployment requires NER (entity extraction over INNs, ATC codes, excipients, posology, contraindication qualifiers) and STS (regulatory equivalence scoring for safety warnings) over SmPCs, PILs, CTD modules, and EU-RMPs. These task categories partially exist in the benchmark (NER, STS) but are calibrated for clinical/research text, not regulatory genres [Q81], [WEB-7].
checklist_responses.IO-2: Regulatory document genres (SmPC, CTD, EU-RMP) are absent as explicit task categories. The benchmark's only confirmed regulatory-genre source is QUAERO EMEA (~10 segmented PIL documents) [WEB-3, WEB-4]. SmPC, CTD module, and EU-RMP text are not documented as data sources [Q34]. Regulatory equivalence as a distinct STS sub-task is absent.
checklist_responses.IO-3: Several included tasks are largely orthogonal to the deployment: FrenchMedMCQA (closed-option exam QA) (FRENCHMEDMCQA-D11, FRENCHMEDMCQA-D13), MORFITT (specialty classification of research abstracts) (MORFITT-D6, MORFITT-D8), DiaMED (ICD-10 chapter classification of African clinical cases) (DIAMED-D1, DIAMED-D9), and CAS/ESSAI POS-only configs (CAS-D13, ESSAI-D18). These contribute to benchmark scores but consume evaluation effort without testing deployment-relevant capabilities.
checklist_responses.IO-4: Documented gaps: no SmPC genre, no CTD module narrative tasks, no EU-RMP tasks, no IDMP-aligned structured-extraction tasks [WEB-16, WEB-17], no regulatory-equivalence STS sub-task. These omit core categories needed for content validity in the EU pharmaceutical regulatory deployment.

evidence_quotes: [Q81] 'aggregating the tasks into one of the five designated categories: POS, NER, MCQA, MCC (Multi-class classification), MLC (Multi-label classification), or STS tasks' (p.8)
[Q83] 'the first large language understanding benchmark tailored for the French biomedical domain' (p.9)
[Q34] 'two text genres (drug leaflets and biomedical titles), consisting of a total of 103,056 words sourced from EMEA or MEDLINE' (p.3)
[Q40] 'Medline (11 classes), and EMEA and Patents (10 classes)' (p.4)
evidence_web_sources: [WEB-3] QUAERO EMEA HuggingFace dataset card confirms PIL register, not SmPC
[WEB-4] QUAERO official corpus page documents EMEA and MEDLINE as the two source genres
[WEB-7] EMA QRD templates page confirms SmPC/PIL templates as the regulatory document standard
[WEB-16] IQVIA NLP regulatory affairs factsheet confirms IDMP structured-extraction requirements
[WEB-17] Linguamatics IDMP blog confirms ~70% of IDMP attributes reside in unstructured text
evidence_dataset: FRENCHMEDMCQA-D11: closed-option recall task produces no NER spans or STS scores
MORFITT-D6: research abstract with no entity-level or similarity ground truth
DIAMED-D1: ICD-10 chapter classification, not NER or STS
CAS-D13: drug INNs annotated only for POS, not regulatory entities
ESSAI-D18: 'effets indésirables' POS-tagged only, not entity-labelled
information_gaps: Whether any French NLP benchmark targeting SmPC/CTD/EU-RMP genres exists outside DrBenchmark — searches found none (gap_id 7 RESOLVED as confirmed gap)
Pair count and source-genre split for DEFT-2020 STS subtask not stated in the paper
requires_expert_verification: Whether the deployment's CTD module supplementary template layer can fully compensate for the absence of CTD-genre evaluation in the benchmark

input_content.justification: While the benchmark contains broad French pharmaceutical lexical coverage (INNs, dosages, routes, formulations) across multiple datasets (QUAERO-D7, ESSAI-D2, DEFT2021-D3, E3C-D1, CAS-D2), the document genres and registers are predominantly clinical case reports, clinical trial protocols, research abstracts, and spoken prescriptions — all distinct from the formal, QRD-template-constrained EMA regulatory register the deployment targets. QUAERO's EMEA sub-corpus is the only confirmed authentic EMA-document source and covers PILs, not SmPCs (~10 segmented documents) [WEB-3, WEB-4]. Multiple datasets contain non-EMA-jurisdiction content from sub-Saharan Africa, North Africa, Canada, and Middle East (DIAMED-D9, MANTRAGSC-D16, MORFITT-D11, MORFITT-D12, MORFITT-D13, E3C-D15, E3C-D16), introducing construct-irrelevant variance. PxCorpus introduces transcribed-speech artifacts (PXCORPUS-D11, PXCORPUS-D13) that have no analogue in regulatory documents. Brand-name predominance over INNs in PxCorpus (PXCORPUS-D19, PXCORPUS-D20) conflicts with EMA INN-primacy requirements. IC is HIGH priority per the elicitation, and the content register and entity-density profile are systematically misaligned with regulatory prose.
strengths: Broad French pharmaceutical vocabulary represented across diverse contexts: INNs, dosages, routes, formulations [Q19], (QUAERO-D7, ESSAI-D2, DEFT2021-D3)
QUAERO EMEA contains authentic EMA leaflet text with authorization headers, full excipient lists with E-numbers, and contraindication statements (QUAERO-D1, QUAERO-D4, QUAERO-D11, QUAERO-D14)
ESSAI clinical trial protocols contain dense INN + posology vocabulary closer to regulatory text than other clinical sources (ESSAI-D2, ESSAI-D4)
DEFT2020 STS includes drug-leaflet sentence pairs with named INNs and safety/contraindication content (DEFT2020-D1, DEFT2020-D2, DEFT2020-D7)

checklist_responses.IC-1: The deployment requires regulatory French in EMA QRD-templated register; the benchmark predominantly contains clinical and research French. The QRD template mandates standardized phrasing 'whenever they are applicable' [WEB-2, WEB-7], but no DrBenchmark corpus is documented as covering this template-constrained register. Sentence-splitting of EMEA documents [Q37] further removes document-level regulatory context (DEFT2020-D21).
checklist_responses.IC-2: EMA/ANSM regulatory culture is well-defined and does not raise sensitive-content concerns; however, the cultural fit issue is reversed — the benchmark contains content from non-EU jurisdictions that dilutes IC validity (DIAMED-D9: Burkina Faso; MORFITT-D11: Saudi Arabia; MORFITT-D12: Egypt; MORFITT-D13: Jordan; MANTRAGSC-D16: Dakar; E3C-D16: Morocco).
checklist_responses.IC-3: The deployment is itself within Western/EU regulatory culture, so the standard 'Western knowledge bias' framing inverts here. The relevant concern is whether the benchmark's content reflects EU-specific regulatory framing — and largely it does not, as much content is research/clinical rather than regulatory (CLISTER-D11, E3C-D11, MORFITT-D8). Brand name vs. INN convention also mismatches EMA INN primacy (PXCORPUS-D19).
checklist_responses.IC-4: INSUFFICIENT DOCUMENTATION — no regional regulatory-affairs annotators or reviewers are documented as having validated content for EMA/ANSM register fit. Would need a regulatory-affairs-led review of the QUAERO EMEA subset and any supplementary corpus to confirm representational fit.
checklist_responses.IC-5: Content issues harming validity: (a) genre register mismatch (clinical/research vs. regulatory), (b) non-EU-jurisdiction content dilution, (c) brand-over-INN bias in PxCorpus, (d) sentence-splitting context loss for long EMEA documents [Q37], (e) absent SmPC/CTD/EU-RMP source text [WEB-3, WEB-4].

[Q34] 'two text genres (drug leaflets and biomedical titles), consisting of a total of 103,056 words sourced from EMEA or MEDLINE' (p.3)
[Q37] 'we decided to split these documents into sentences' (p.3)
[Q44] '4 hours (1,981 recordings) of transcribed and annotated dialogues focused on drug prescriptions' (p.4)

[WEB-3] QUAERO HuggingFace confirms EMEA = patient leaflet text
[WEB-4] QUAERO official corpus page documents 103,056 words across drug leaflets and biomedical titles
[WEB-7] EMA confirms SmPC/PIL templates as the regulatory document standard

QUAERO-D4: full excipient list with E-numbers in SmPC Section 6 format
ESSAI-D2: pemetrexed/cisplatine/carboplatine combination regimen with cycle count

MORFITT-D11: Saudi Arabian medical training context — outside EMA scope
MORFITT-D12: Egyptian Arabic-language study, non-EMA
E3C-D16: Moroccan patient origin — non-EMA jurisdictional context
DEFT2020-D21: posology decontextualized by sentence splitting — section/drug identity lost

No regulatory-affairs annotator review of QUAERO EMEA subset for SmPC/PIL register fit
requires_expert_verification: Whether the QUAERO EMEA subset is sufficiently representative of current QRD v10.4 phrasing conventions [WEB-7] for the deployment's needs
Whether the deployment's supplementary template layer for CTD modules can compensate for the absence of CTD-genre training/evaluation content

input_form.justification: Both the deployment and the benchmark operate on French text in Latin script with standard diacritics; no script, modality, or infrastructure mismatch exists. The benchmark's HuggingFace toolkit and tokenization analysis [Q48, Q89, Q90] support transferability. The minor concerns are (a) sentence-splitting of long EMEA documents [Q37] which strips structural context relevant to long SmPC/CTD documents, and (b) PxCorpus's transcribed-speech register (PXCORPUS-D11, PXCORPUS-D13) which is incompatible with regulatory written prose. IF is LOWER priority per the elicitation, and these issues do not threaten core form-level validity.
strengths: French Latin-script text consistent with deployment environment across all datasets [Q31]
HuggingFace Datasets toolkit with normalized loaders supports reproducibility [Q48]
Tokenization study shows segmentation differences have minor impact on performance [Q78, Q79, Q80]
Hyperparameters fully documented for reproducibility [Q49, Q99]

checklist_responses.IF-1: No signal-distribution mismatch — both deployment and benchmark are written French text in Latin script [Q31]. Tokenization variance was studied and found to have minor impact [Q79, Q80].
checklist_responses.IF-2: Regional infrastructure (EU pharmaceutical companies, ANSM/EMA) is text-only enterprise document processing, fully compatible with the benchmark's text format [Q48].
checklist_responses.IF-3: Two domain-specific form differences: (a) Long-document handling: SmPCs/CTDs exceed transformer input limits; benchmark mitigates via sentence-splitting [Q37], but this strips section/cross-reference context (DEFT2020-D21, QUAERO-D22, QUAERO-D24). (b) PxCorpus transcribed speech (PXCORPUS-D11, PXCORPUS-D13) introduces disfluencies and English fragments not present in regulatory documents.
checklist_responses.IF-4: Form mismatches are minor: sentence-splitting context loss for long regulatory documents, and the PxCorpus speech-transcription register being inapplicable to formal regulatory prose. No script, encoding, or modality issues.

evidence_quotes: [Q31] 'only the French portion is retained for the benchmark' (p.3)
[Q37] 'we decided to split these documents into sentences' (p.3)
[Q48] 'a practical toolkit based on the HuggingFace Datasets library' (p.5)
[Q79] 'DrBERT-CP outperforms FlauBERT on 16 out of the 20 tasks, thus contradicting previous conclusions' (p.8)
[Q80] 'tokenization, as it is currently done in MLMs, seems to play a minor role in the performance of systems' (p.8)
evidence_dataset: PXCORPUS-D11: English fragments, expletives, off-task speech — absent from regulatory text
PXCORPUS-D13: transcribed system error message — impossible in written regulatory documents
DEFT2020-D21: posology decontextualized by sentence splitting
QUAERO-D22: single-character fragment from sentence splitting
QUAERO-D25: Czech-language text embedded in French EMEA document due to multilingual leaflet structure
requires_expert_verification: Whether sentence-splitting context loss materially impairs SmPC/CTD compliance verification in deployment

output_ontology.justification: This is the most severely misaligned dimension. The deployment requires (a) NER over regulatory entity types — INN (distinct from CHEM), ATC code, excipient name, EMA-templated posology, contraindication qualifier, safety warning — and (b) STS calibrated for regulatory equivalence sensitive to dose-threshold and contraindicated-population qualifier differences. The benchmark provides neither. NER schemas use clinical UMLS Semantic Groups (QUAERO 10 classes [Q35, Q103]; Mantra-GSC 10–11 classes [Q106]; E3C clinical entities [Q104]; DEFT2021 13 clinical types) and clinical PxCorpus 38 classes [Q44, Q108], none of which include INN-vs-CHEM distinction, ATC codes, or contraindication qualifiers (QUAERO-D16, QUAERO-D17, QUAERO-D21, MANTRAGSC-D15, PXCORPUS-D15). Dose values systematically receive O tags in QUAERO (QUAERO-D18, QUAERO-D19) and E3C (E3C-D7, E3C-D8). STS is calibrated for general semantic proximity [Q41, Q54]; CLISTER scores a 2× numeric difference (10 vs. 20 mmol/l) at 4.0 (CLISTER-D13), DEFT2020 scores a dropped 'congénitale' qualifier at 4.6 (DEFT2020-D18) and a specific-substance vs. generic-term swap at 4.3 (DEFT2020-D20). The deployment explicitly treats such differences as critical mismatches (A3). External evidence (Kadi et al. 2025 [WEB-11]) confirms ATC and excipient extraction from SmPCs is unsolved field-wide. OO is HIGH priority per the elicitation, and the fundamental decision rule for STS and the entity inventory for NER are both misaligned.
strengths: QUAERO CHEM class captures pharmaceutical substances including INNs and excipients at a coarse level, providing partial signal (QUAERO-D7, QUAERO-D15)
PxCorpus NER schema includes DOSAGE, MODE, FREQUENCY, DURATION, SUBSTANCE [Q108] — the closest approach to regulatory posology entity labels
DEFT2021 NER includes treatment, dosage, mode, and frequency entity types (DEFT2021-D5, DEFT2021-D6) — partial pharmaceutical entity coverage
STS uses graded 0–5 scale with multiple annotators averaged [Q41], providing variance signal (DEFT2020-D10, DEFT2020-D11) even if calibration is wrong

checklist_responses.OO-1: Benchmark NER label categories (UMLS Semantic Groups: GEOG, PHEN, DISO, ANAT, OBJC, PHYS, PROC, DEVI, CHEM, LIVB [Q103]; PxCorpus 38 clinical-prescription classes [Q108]) are calibrated for clinical research, not EU regulatory entity extraction. STS is general semantic similarity [Q54], not regulatory equivalence (CLISTER-D13).
checklist_responses.OO-2: Missing categories specific to the regional context: INN as distinct from CHEM (QUAERO-D15, QUAERO-D17, MANTRAGSC-D15), ATC codes [WEB-11], excipient names as distinct from CHEM, EMA-templated posology expressions, contraindication qualifiers (QUAERO-D21, PXCORPUS-D15), safety warning categories. The 2025 Kadi et al. study confirms ATC and excipient extraction from SmPCs is field-wide unresolved [WEB-11].
checklist_responses.OO-3: Categories not strictly 'non-regional' since both deployment and benchmark are in EU/French context, but UMLS Semantic Groups encode a clinical-research ontology rather than a regulatory ontology. Conflation of INN with general CHEM (QUAERO-D17) misrepresents regulatory entity distinctions critical under EMA QRD requirements [WEB-8].
checklist_responses.OO-4: Stakeholder-driven taxonomy redesign is required: regulatory-affairs experts would need to define INN/ATC/excipient/posology/contraindication-qualifier entity types and a regulatory-equivalence STS rubric calibrated for dose-threshold and population-qualifier sensitivity. No such schema exists in published French NLP literature (gap_id 3 RESOLVED as confirmed gap).
checklist_responses.OO-5: Taxonomy issues harming structural and content validity: (a) NER schemas conflate INN, excipient, brand name under CHEM; (b) no dose/posology entity class in QUAERO despite abundant dose content; (c) no contraindication qualifier class anywhere; (d) STS scoring rubric demonstrably treats legally material differences as near-synonymy (CLISTER-D13, DEFT2020-D18, DEFT2020-D20).

evidence_quotes: [Q35] '10 entity categories corresponding to the UMLS Semantic Groups' (p.3)
[Q103] 'O, GEOG, PHEN, DISO, ANAT, OBJC, PHYS, PROC, DEVI, CHEM and LIVB' (p.14)
[Q41] 'similarity scores ranging from 0 to 5' (p.4)
[Q54] 'Spearman correlation, and (2) the mean relative solution distance accuracy (EDRM)' (p.6)
[Q82] 'the leading model, DrBERT-FS, does not excel in tasks such as MLC or STS' (p.8)
[Q108] 'O, ANATOMY, DATE, DOSAGE, DURATION, MEDICAL EXAM, FREQUENCY, MODE, MOMENT, PATHOLOGY, SOSY, SUBSTANCE, TREATMENT and VALUE' (p.14)

[WEB-11] Kadi et al. 2025 confirms ATC codes and excipient dosages 'frequently missing or misclassified' even with state-of-the-art LLMs on EMA SmPCs

QUAERO-D17: INN in pharmacokinetic sentence; CHEM conflates API with other chemical entities
QUAERO-D18: dose value '21,6 microgrammes' receives O tag — dosage not a labeled class
QUAERO-D21: contraindication qualifier present but no contraindication entity in schema
MANTRAGSC-D15: testostérone and prolactine both tagged CHEM — INN/endogenous distinction absent
E3C-D7: four INN drugs with dosages all O-tagged
PXCORPUS-D15: contraindication qualifier 'si absence d'ulcère' tagged O
CLISTER-D13: 2× numerical difference (10 vs. 20 mmol/l) scored 4.0 — regulatory-critical treated as near-equivalent
DEFT2020-D18: 'congénitale' qualifier dropped, scored 4.6
DEFT2020-D20: specific substance name vs. generic term scored 4.3
information_gaps: No published STS rubric calibrated for regulatory equivalence in any language (gap_id 3 RESOLVED as confirmed field-wide gap)
No published comparison of regulatory-affairs annotator judgments vs. biomedical NLP annotator judgments on EMA-format text
requires_expert_verification: Whether the deployment's regulatory-specific template layer can be configured to compensate for the absent INN/ATC/excipient entity types when running the benchmark-trained model as a foundational extraction layer
Quantitative threshold for STS regulatory-equivalence calibration that the deployment requires

output_content.justification: Annotation provenance is poorly documented across the benchmark. Only three datasets have explicit annotation documentation: DEFT-2021 (manual annotation, no annotator profiles) [Q25], CLISTER (multiple annotators, profiles undocumented) [Q41], and DiaMed (several annotators including one medical expert, but for ICD-10 classification only — not NER or STS) [Q45]. CAS POS was automatically annotated via Tagex 3 [Q42]. Author affiliations are academic NLP and clinical medicine institutions [Q8] — no regulatory-affairs or pharmacovigilance expertise is documented. The deployment explicitly anticipates systematic disagreement between biomedical NLP annotators and regulatory-standard judgments on borderline cases (A4). Empirical evidence in dataset analysis confirms this: high annotator variance on pharmaceutical pairs (DEFT2020-D10: scores [5,2,4,4,5]; DEFT2020-D11; DEFT2020-D22 [5,3,3,4,3]) shows non-specialist annotation; E3C systematically labels disease entities while leaving drug names unlabeled (E3C-D9, E3C-D14); QUAERO annotators were NLP/biomedical researchers mapping to UMLS, not regulatory affairs specialists (QUAERO-D20). OC is HIGH priority per the elicitation. The DiaMed inter-annotator agreement reporting [Q46] is a structural strength but does not address regulatory norms.
strengths: DiaMed reports inter-annotator agreement (Kappa Cohen, Gwet's AC1) computed across two annotation sessions [Q45, Q46]
CLISTER averages multiple annotator scores producing graded ground truth [Q41], enabling variance analysis (DEFT2020-D10)
CAS POS automatic annotations were validated against manual annotations at 98% precision [Q42]
DiaMed includes one medical expert annotator [Q45] — partial domain expertise for the ICD-10 task

checklist_responses.OC-1: Ground truth labels do NOT reflect regional regulatory stakeholder perspectives. No annotation guidelines reference EMA/ANSM regulatory standards. The deployment's authoritative ground truth rests with regulatory-affairs specialists, but no benchmark dataset documents such annotators (QUAERO-D20, CLISTER-D16).
checklist_responses.OC-2: Systematic disagreement is empirically expected: annotator variance on pharmaceutical STS pairs (DEFT2020-D10 [5,2,4,4,5]; DEFT2020-D22 [5,3,3,4,3]) demonstrates non-specialist annotation; E3C consistently labels diseases while leaving drug names O (E3C-D9, E3C-D14) reflects clinical-priority norms divergent from regulatory; A4 from elicitation explicitly anticipates this gap.
checklist_responses.OC-3: Annotator demographics are NOT DOCUMENTED for the majority of corpora. CLISTER says 'multiple annotators' [Q41] without profiles; DiaMed lists 'several annotators including one medical expert' [Q45]; QUAERO, E3C, PxCorpus, ESSAI annotator profiles are not documented in the paper. No Datasheets-style demographic disclosures exist.
checklist_responses.OC-4: Re-annotation by regulatory-affairs annotators would be required for deployment-grade label quality. No such re-annotation effort is documented in DrBenchmark or in any published French NLP corpus (gap_id 4 RESOLVED as confirmed gap).
checklist_responses.OC-5: Aggregation by averaging in CLISTER [Q41] erases minority/dissenting judgments — visible as variance pattern (DEFT2020-D10). For regulatory equivalence, dissenting annotators flagging legal mismatch would be the signal of interest, but the averaging method suppresses it.
checklist_responses.OC-6: Label issues harming convergent and external validity: (a) annotator population systematically misaligned with deployment ground-truth source; (b) no EMA/ANSM annotation guidelines documented; (c) variance suppression via averaging in STS; (d) consistent clinical-priority annotation norms in NER (E3C-D9, QUAERO-D18); (e) no documented re-annotation effort exists for regulatory norms.

Whether DiaMed's medical expert participation translates to any regulatory-affairs annotation norm overlap

output_form.justification: Output representations (IOB2 NER labels, multi-label classification, multiple-choice indices, continuous 0–5 STS scores [Q52, Q53, Q54]) match the deployment's consumption modalities. The deployment ingests entity spans and STS scores; both are produced by the benchmark in compatible formats. Statistical reporting (averaging over four runs, Student's t-test [Q51], majority-class baseline [Q56]) supports interpretable results. SeqEval with first-token prediction [Q52, Q53] provides tokenizer-agnostic evaluation. The minor concerns are (a) the EDRM metric's specificity to DEFT-2020 [Q54] without documented calibration for regulatory micro-differences, and (b) the ~2,500 GPU-hour reproducibility cost [Q92, Q93] for users without HPC access. OF is LOWER priority per the elicitation.
strengths: Standard NER output via SeqEval IOB2 with tokenizer-agnostic first-token prediction [Q52, Q53]
Continuous 0–5 STS scores in a format directly consumable by the deployment's compliance flagging logic [Q41, Q54]
Robust statistical reporting: four runs averaged, Student's t-test [Q51], majority-class baseline [Q56]
Best-model selection via validation metric mitigates overfitting [Q100]
Hyperparameters documented for reproducibility [Q49, Q99]

checklist_responses.OF-1: Output modalities (IOB2 entity spans, classification labels, continuous STS scores) match deployment needs [Q52, Q54]. The deployment ingests entity spans for NER and continuous scores for STS — both are produced by the benchmark.
checklist_responses.OF-2: Not applicable — the deployment is text-only enterprise document processing; no text-to-speech requirements (deployment infrastructure_notes).
checklist_responses.OF-3: Not applicable — the target population is highly literate professional regulatory affairs specialists; no accessibility constraints relevant to output form.
checklist_responses.OF-4: Minor form concerns: (a) EDRM metric specific to DEFT-2020 task design [Q54] has no documented calibration for regulatory equivalence, but this is a calibration concern (OO) rather than a representation mismatch; (b) reproducibility burden of 2,500 GPU hours [Q92, Q93] limits independent re-running but does not affect output-form compatibility.

evidence_quotes: [Q52] 'SeqEval (Nakayama, 2018) metric in conjunction with the IOB2 format' (p.6)
[Q53] 'tokenizer-agnostic evaluation' (p.6)
[Q54] 'Spearman correlation, and (2) the mean relative solution distance accuracy (EDRM)' (p.6)
[Q51] 'averaging the scores from four separate runs ... statistical significance computed using Student's t-test' (p.5)
[Q92] 'a substantial amount of computational power to execute all runs' (p.9)
[Q93] 'approximately 2,500 hours on V100 GPUs' (p.9)

overall_summary: DrBenchmark is the largest French biomedical NLU benchmark and provides genuine value for general French biomedical model selection [Q83], but it is severely misaligned with the EU pharmaceutical regulatory NLP deployment on the four HIGH-priority dimensions identified in the elicitation. Output Ontology (score 1) is the most critical concern: NER schemas use clinical UMLS Semantic Groups that conflate INN, excipients, and brand names under CHEM (QUAERO-D15, MANTRAGSC-D15), provide no ATC code class [WEB-11], no contraindication qualifier class (QUAERO-D21, PXCORPUS-D15), and systematically O-tag dose values (QUAERO-D18, E3C-D7); STS is calibrated for general semantic proximity rather than regulatory equivalence, demonstrably treating legally material differences as near-synonymy (CLISTER-D13: 2× numeric difference scored 4.0; DEFT2020-D18: dropped 'congénitale' scored 4.6). Input Ontology (score 2) and Input Content (score 2) reflect the absence of SmPC, CTD module, and EU-RMP genres — QUAERO EMEA's ~10 segmented PIL documents [WEB-3, WEB-4] are the only confirmed regulatory-genre source. Output Content (score 2) reflects systematically misaligned annotator populations (academic NLP and clinical, no regulatory-affairs specialists [Q8]) with empirical variance evidence (DEFT2020-D10, DEFT2020-D22). Input Form (score 4) and Output Form (score 4) are well-aligned — both deployment and benchmark are text-only French Latin-script with compatible output representations. The benchmark can support model selection at the foundational French-biomedical-LM level, but cannot validate deployment-grade regulatory NER or regulatory-equivalence STS without substantial supplementation.

practical_guidance.what_this_benchmark_measures: DrBenchmark measures general French biomedical language understanding across NER, STS, classification, MCQA, and POS tagging on clinical, research, and pharmacy-exam text. For this deployment, it provides useful signal on (a) which French biomedical MLM has the best general performance profile (DrBERT-FS, DrBERT-CP, CamemBERT-bio per [Q60–Q62]), (b) coarse-grained pharmaceutical NER on EMEA-genre patient leaflets via QUAERO (QUAERO-D1–D15), and (c) baseline French biomedical STS variance behavior via CLISTER and DEFT2020. The strongest dimensions for this deployment are Input Form and Output Form — both at score 4.
practical_guidance.construct_depth: The benchmark probes general French biomedical NLU constructs at moderate depth, but does not probe the regulatory-specific constructs the deployment requires. NER probes clinical entity recognition (UMLS Semantic Groups, MeSH chapters, ICD-10 chapters) — not regulatory entity recognition (INN as distinct from CHEM, ATC codes, excipient names, EMA-templated posology, contraindication qualifiers). STS probes general clinical sentence similarity — not regulatory equivalence. Empirically, pharmaceutical content is broadly present in the inputs but is systematically unannotated or under-annotated (E3C-D7, QUAERO-D18, CAS-D13). The benchmark's 75% top-task win rate for domain-specialized models [Q62] does not translate to regulatory-grade precision (per [WEB-12]: general biomedical NLP systems require modification for regulatory use).
practical_guidance.supplementation_needed: To complete this assessment for regulatory deployment, the user needs: (1) An OO supplement — a regulatory entity schema (INN, ATC, excipient, posology template, contraindication qualifier) and a regulatory-equivalence STS rubric calibrated for dose-threshold and population-qualifier sensitivity; neither exists in published literature (gap_id 3 confirmed). (2) An IO/IC supplement — a held-out evaluation set of authentic SmPC, CTD module narrative, and EU-RMP text under QRD v10.4 [WEB-7], since QUAERO EMEA covers only PIL register and ~10 segmented documents. (3) An OC supplement — re-annotation of a sample of QUAERO EMEA and CLISTER pairs by regulatory-affairs specialists to quantify the gap between biomedical NLP annotation norms and EMA/ANSM judgments (anticipated per A4). (4) Empirical evaluation against the Kadi et al. 2025 SmPC IDMP extraction baseline [WEB-11] for ATC and excipient extraction, which is field-wide unsolved.

remediation_suggestions.gap: NER schemas conflate INN, excipients, and brand names under CHEM; no ATC code, contraindication qualifier, or EMA-templated posology entity types; STS scoring treats legally material qualifier differences as near-synonymy.
remediation_suggestions.recommendation: Construct a deployment-side regulatory entity schema (INN distinct from CHEM, ATC, excipient, posology template fields, contraindication qualifier, safety warning) and re-annotate a held-out QUAERO EMEA sample under this schema. Develop a complementary regulatory-equivalence STS rubric with explicit rules for dose-threshold and population-qualifier mismatches, and validate the deployment's STS layer against this rubric rather than against CLISTER/DEFT2020 scores alone.

remediation_suggestions.gap: SmPC, CTD module, and EU-RMP document genres are absent as task categories; only QUAERO EMEA (~10 segmented PIL documents) provides confirmed regulatory-genre coverage.
remediation_suggestions.recommendation: Use QUAERO EMEA only as a partial sanity check for PIL-register NER. For SmPC/CTD/EU-RMP genres, construct a deployment-internal evaluation corpus from authentic regulatory submissions under current QRD v10.4 [WEB-7] and treat DrBenchmark performance as a model-selection proxy, not a deployment-readiness measure.

remediation_suggestions.gap: Non-EU-jurisdiction content (sub-Saharan Africa, North Africa, Middle East, Canada) dilutes IC validity; brand-name predominance in PxCorpus conflicts with EMA INN primacy; sentence-splitting strips long-document context.
remediation_suggestions.recommendation: When fine-tuning on DrBenchmark, weight or filter to prioritize the QUAERO EMEA, Mantra-GSC EMEA/Patents, and ESSAI subsets that are closest to EU regulatory content. Consider augmenting with deployment-internal SmPC/CTD/RMP text. Develop a long-document handling strategy beyond simple sentence-splitting (e.g., section-aware chunking) for SmPC/CTD inputs.

remediation_suggestions.gap: No annotation guidelines reference EMA/ANSM standards; annotator populations are academic NLP and clinical, with no documented regulatory-affairs expertise; STS averaging suppresses dissent that would signal regulatory mismatch.
remediation_suggestions.recommendation: Commission a pilot re-annotation of 200–500 regulatory-relevant items (QUAERO EMEA NER, CLISTER and DEFT2020 STS pairs containing dose/contraindication content) by regulatory-affairs specialists. Quantify the disagreement rate with benchmark labels on borderline cases. Use this to bound expected error in deployment and to calibrate the human-review flagging threshold.

remediation_suggestions.gap: STS scoring rubric demonstrably treats 2× numerical differences (CLISTER-D13) and dropped legal qualifiers (DEFT2020-D18) as near-synonymy, opposite of the deployment's regulatory-equivalence requirement.
remediation_suggestions.recommendation: Do not rely on CLISTER or DEFT2020 STS scores as the primary signal for the deployment's compliance-flagging layer. Build a small adversarial regulatory-equivalence test set with paired examples differing only in dose threshold, qualifier, or population scope, and use it as a deployment-internal validation gate before relying on any STS-based compliance check.

remediation_suggestions.gap: Sentence-splitting of long EMEA documents [Q37] strips section structure that is legally and semantically critical in SmPC/CTD compliance verification.
remediation_suggestions.recommendation: When applying DrBenchmark-trained models to deployment SmPC/CTD documents, implement section-aware chunking that preserves QRD section identity (4.1 Indications, 4.2 Posology, 4.3 Contraindications, 4.4 Special warnings) as metadata accompanying each chunk, so downstream regulatory rules can use section context that the benchmark training did not preserve.
